heading: PER MEZZO DELLE INTELLIGENZE DELLE COSE
card_summary: This chunk opens the section "Per mezzo delle intelligenze delle cose" (Through the Intelligences of Things) in the Third Canticle, Second Tone of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. Chapter 1 examines the nature of the soul, rejecting Aristotle's two definitions in favour of a Platonic-Pythagorean account: the soul is a divine substance emanating from divine sources, bearing a rational number (not the divine number of the Demiurge) through which it stands in proportional harmony with all things and thus comprehends them intellectually. Zorzi cites Pythagoras's Aurea Carmina, Paul quoting Aratus (Acts 17), John 1:3-4, Aristotle's "the soul is in a certain way all things," and Augustine on innate exemplars. Chapter 2 turns to whether God created all souls equal, pitting Peripatetic equality against the Parisian Scotist thesis of intrinsic grades of being; Zorzi supports gradation with Matthew 25 (the parable of the talents), Wisdom 8:19-20, Gregory, Origen, and a sustained analogy from musical proportion (tone, semitone, lemma, diesis in sesquioctave ratio 9:8). The summary covers the opening portion of this long section (approximately pages 2099-2168 as presented).
essay:
## Overview

Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this very long chunk (approximately the first 300 lines / ~6,000 words as read), corresponding to Chapters 1 and 2 of the section "Per mezzo delle intelligenze delle cose" in the Third Canticle, Second Tone of *De Harmonia Mundi*. The full section extends to page 2168 and likely contains additional chapters not captured here.

The section's governing question is: how does the soul stand in a relationship of sympathetic proportion to the entire order of reality such that it can comprehend all things intellectually? This is framed as a continuation of earlier discussions of the soul's consonance with body and with virtue, now extended to its consonance with "tutte le cose che comprende intellettualmente."

---

## Chapter 1: The Nature of the Soul (*Cos'è l'anima*)

### Rejection of Aristotle

Zorzi opens by setting aside Aristotle's two standard definitions of soul. The first — the soul as "atto del corpo naturale organico che ha la vita in potenza" (act of an organic natural body having life in potency) — explains only the soul's role in bodily motion but says nothing about its capacity to comprehend all things. The second — "l'anima è il principio della sensazione, dell'intelligenza e del movimento locale" — is scarcely better, indicating that the soul is the principle of understanding but not explaining how it can encompass all things intellectually. Zorzi absolves Aristotle partly, noting that even Plato in the tenth book of the *Laws* admitted: "Quasi tutti sembrano aver ignorato che cosa sia l'anima e quale sia la sua potenza." Proclus too, Zorzi observes, though he refuted many interpretations of Plato on the soul, himself declined to reveal Plato's true opinion, leaving it for careful readers to infer — in the manner of "i teologi esoterici" (esoteric theologians).

### The Platonic-Pythagorean Soul as Divine Substance with Rational Number

From the Platonic tradition Zorzi draws the positive doctrine: the soul is a divine substance (*divina quaedam substantia*) emanating from divine sources (*a divinis fontibus emanans*). Crucially it bears within itself a number — not the divine number by which the Demiurge ordered creation, but a **rational number** (*numerus rationalis*) through which the soul stands in proportion to all things and can therefore comprehend them all intellectually. The Latin text is explicit:

> *ducens secum numerum, non quippe illum divinum, quo opifex omnia disposuit, sed rationalem numerum, quo cum omnibus proportionem habens, omnia intelligere possit.*

<cite>p. 2099 / Latin facing-text, line 28-29</cite>

This claim that the soul is "something divine" is buttressed by two authorities: (1) Pythagoras in the *Aurea Carmina* — "Abbi fiducia, perché gli uomini hanno un'origine divina" (*Confide quia divinum genus hominibus inest*) — and (2) Paul in Acts 17:28, citing Aratus: "Poiché di lui stirpe noi siamo" (*Ipsius enim genus sumus*). The scriptural warrant from Acts is especially important to Zorzi as it gives apostolic authority to the Pythagorean-Platonic position.

### Aristoxenus, Xenocrates, and Self-Moving Number

Zorzi then engages Aristoxenus and Xenocrates, who defined the soul as a self-moving number (*numerum seipsum moventem*). Aristotle's refutation of this, Zorzi contends, rests on an equivocation: Aristoxenus and Xenocrates speak of formal and rational number, while Aristotle speaks of material, mercantile number. Similarly, the motion in question is the soul's proper self-motion (as in free will and intellection), not the mechanical local motion of bodies dividing space part by part.

Zorzi invokes Augustine: "Nulla è altrettanto in potere della volontà quanto la volontà stessa" (*Nihil est tam in potestate voluntatis quam ipsa voluntas*), showing that the soul (as will) moves itself freely. He also cites Job on the instability of angels — "tra i suoi angeli ha posto il movimento" — reading the Hebrew text against the Vulgate's mistaken "trova la malvagità," to show that even angelic intelligences are characterised by a true self-motion.

### The Soul as Containing the Species of All Things

Aristotle's own admission — *anima est quodammodo omnia* ("l'anima è in qualche modo tutte le cose") — is read by Zorzi as meaning that the soul contains all things in the mode of understanding, or bears a proportional relationship to all things. This is the *harmonia rationalis* that Zorzi identifies as the common property he shares with the Pythagoreans and Platonists.

The soul contains the species and numbers of all intelligible things not as adventitious acquisitions but as innate (*congenii ipsi animae*), following the Platonic-Academic doctrine. The authority is John 1:3-4:

> *Quod factum est, in ipso vita erat*

<cite>p. 2099 / col. XVIIr of the Latin text</cite>

— life in God is creative and divine, and the exemplars of all things exist more perfectly in the divine intellect than in their own genera. Augustine's parallel doctrine on the exemplars of artefacts in the mind of the craftsman is invoked. The soul is thus, for Zorzi, an *intellectivum quoddam omnia continens concinnio quodam* — an intellectual being containing all things in a kind of symmetry or concordance.

The soul nonetheless falls far short of the First and Supreme Intellect, which comprehends all things simultaneously in a single act; the soul requires many successive acts of understanding.

---

## Chapter 2: Whether the Craftsman Created All Souls Equal (*An omnes animas aequales creaverit opifex*)

### The Peripatetic Position and Its Scholastic Correction

The Peripatetics held that all souls were produced at equal grades, following Aristotle's dictum that species behave like numbers — add one unit and you get a different species. But the more recent Parisian theologians (the Scotists) decreed in their articles that souls differ not merely by accidents but by intrinsic grades (*gradus intrinseci*) rooted in their essence — the *haecceitas* principle. Specifically, they determined that anyone who claimed the soul of Peter was not more perfect by its intrinsic grade than the soul of Judas would be in error.

Zorzi aligns with the Scotist position. He cites John Duns Scotus (*Iohanne Scoto*) who held in his *Quaestiones theologicae* that each thing is first distinguished by its own nature — "questo," its thisness — before being distinguished by principles of operation or accidental qualities.

### Scriptural and Patristic Support for Soul-Grades

The key scriptural proof-texts are:

> *Puer eram ingeniosus, et sortitus sum animam bonam* (Wisdom 8:19-20)

— "I was an ingenious child and I was allotted a good soul" — i.e., better than that of many others. And Matthew 25:15:

> *Uni dedit quinque talenta, alii duo, alii vero unum, unicuique secundum propriam virtutem.*

<cite>p. 2099 / col. XVIIv of the Latin text</cite>

Zorzi asks: if the talents (divine gifts) are distributed "according to each person's own capacity (*propriam virtutem*)," what is that capacity if not a natural power with a determinate grade? It cannot itself be a gratuitous gift, since the gifts are distributed according to it. This natural capacity is the soul's intrinsic grade.

Gregory and Origen are cited in support: Gregory holds that angels and humans converge in their natural gifts and grades; Origen speaks of invisible powers with differences no less great than those found among humans.

### The Musical Analogy for Graduated Unity

Zorzi addresses the Aristotelian objection from number-species by arguing that even the musical unit (*unitas musica*) can be divided into unequal parts. The tone stands in sesquioctave proportion (9:8). By scaling up to 18:16, the unit can be split into two semitones (18 to 17, and 17 to 16). Scaling further to 36:32 yields four *dieses*. The musical unit thus contains hidden internal grades (*occulti gradus*) even while remaining a formal unity:

> *Est quidem tonus in proportione sexquioctava, sicuti 9 ad 8, ubi 9 excedit 8 per unitatem, quae facit ipsum tonum. Tonus autem dividitur in semitonia, lemmata, et dieses.*

<cite>p. 2099 / col. XVIIIr of the Latin text</cite>

The same logic applies to the *numerus naturalis* — the natural proportion governing things — where unity can be divided in the same way. Jerome is then cited (*vires* as the individualising grades), though the passage breaks off at the end of the read portion.

---

## Technical Terms and Key Concepts

- *numerus rationalis* / *numerus formalis*: formal-rational number, contrasted with material/mercantile number
- *harmonia rationalis*: rational harmony — the soul's proportional relationship to all things
- *gradus intrinseci*: Scotist intrinsic grades of being rooted in the soul's essence
- *haecceitas* (implied by "questo"): Scotist principle of individual thisness
- *unitas musica*: musical unity, capable of internal gradation into semitone (*semitonium*), lemma (*lemma*), diesis (*diesis*)
- *proportio sexquioctava* (9:8): the sesquioctave ratio defining a whole tone

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section sits at the heart of Zorzi's cosmological programme. The thesis that the soul bears a *numerus rationalis* structurally analogous to the order of creation is the epistemological foundation of the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*: the world is knowable because the soul is numerically proportioned to it. The rejection of Aristotelian definition in favour of Platonic-Pythagorean emanationism, supported by Paul, John, and Augustine, is characteristic of Zorzi's Christian Neoplatonism. The Scotist doctrine of intrinsic soul-grades integrates scholastic theology into the harmonic scheme, ensuring that the diversity of creation — from the highest intelligences to the humblest human soul — reflects an ordered, graduated emanation from the divine source, as the musical analogy of tone, semitone, lemma, and diesis makes architecturally vivid.
